column1: # Column: english_word — case-insensitive match against collection_words.english_word

column2: max ~120 chars; stored as plain text
Old Norse 'geta' (to obtain); entered English c. 1200
Old Norse 'taka' (to grasp); replaced Old English 'niman'
Old Norse 'vanta' (to lack); meaning shifted to 'desire' c. 1400
Old Norse 'sœma' (to conform to); meaning 'appear' emerged c. 1200
Old English 'laefan' (to let remain); from Proto-Germanic *laibijaną
Old English 'sceawian' (to look at); meaning 'display' by 1200
Old English 'losian' (to perish/be lost); from Proto-Germanic *luzaną
Old French 'payer' (to appease); from Latin 'pacare' (to pacify)
Old English 'waeccan' (to be awake); from Proto-Germanic *wakōną
Old English 'stoppian' (to plug/block); from Latin 'stuppa' (tow fiber)
Old English 'spendan'; from Latin 'expendere' (to weigh out)
Old English 'winnan' (to labor/fight); meaning 'be victorious' by 1300
Old English 'offrian' (to sacrifice); from Latin 'offerre' (to present)
Old French 'remembrer'; from Latin 'rememorari' (to call to mind)
Old French 'aparoir'; from Latin 'apparere' (to be visible)
Old French 'servir'; from Latin 'servire' (to be a slave/serve)
Latin 'exspectare' (to look out for); from 'ex-' + 'spectare' (to look)
Old French 'ester' (to stand); from Latin 'stare' (to stand)
Middle English 'cutten'; possibly Old Norse origin; replacing Old English 'ceorfan'
Old English 'sellan' (to give/hand over); meaning 'sell' by 800s
Old French 'retourner'; from Latin 're-' + 'tornare' (to turn on a lathe)
Old French 'agreer' (to please); from Latin 'ad gratum' (to one's liking)
Latin 'problema'; from Greek 'problema' (obstacle); from 'proballein' (to throw forward)
Old French 'moneie'; from Latin 'moneta' (mint); from Juno Moneta's temple
Old English 'scol'; from Latin 'schola'; from Greek 'skhole' (leisure/learning)
Old French 'pooir'; from Latin 'potere' (to be able)
Latin 'systema'; from Greek 'systema' (whole composed of parts)
Latin 'programma'; from Greek 'programma' (public notice); from 'prographein' (to write publicly)
Old French 'nature'; from Latin 'natura' (birth/character); from 'nasci' (to be born)
Old French 'science'; from Latin 'scientia' (knowledge); from 'scire' (to know)
Latin 'possibilis' (that can be done); from 'posse' (to be able)
Latin 'largus' (abundant); meaning 'big' entered English via French
Middle English 'badde'; origin uncertain; possibly Old English 'baeddel' (hermaphrodite)
Latin 'privatus' (withdrawn from public life); from 'privare' (to deprive)
Old English 'freo' (acting of free will); from Proto-Germanic *frijaz (free)
Old French 'commun'; from Latin 'communis' (shared by all)
Medieval Latin 'availabilis'; from Old French 'availer' (to be of use)
